content.text: Write solutions in a neat and logical fashion, giving complete reasons for all steps. You must attempt SIX problems.

body.text: State Tonelli’s theorem, and give an example to show that the \(\sigma\)-finiteness hypothesis is necessary.
Give an example of each of the following, if possible. (If not possible, give a brief explanation of why.) When giving examples, be clear about what measure space you are working in.

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text: a sequence of functions converging in measure but not in \(L^1\),

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: a sequence converging almost uniformly but not essentially uniformly,

subparts.label: c)
subparts.text: a sequence converging in \(L^1\) but not almost everywhere.

body.text: Let \((X,\mathcal{M},\mu)\) be a measure space. Prove that \(L^2(\mu)\) is complete (in the \(L^2\) norm).
Let \(\mathcal{X}\) be a Banach space. Prove that every finite-dimensional subspace \(\mathcal{M}\subset\mathcal{X}\) is closed.

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: State and prove a version of the chain rule for Radon–Nikodym derivatives.

body.text: Let \((X,\mathcal{M},\mu)\) be a \(\sigma\)-finite measure space, let \(1\leq q<p<\infty\) and suppose that \(L^q(\mu)\subseteq L^p(\mu)\). Prove that there exists a constant \(c>0\) such that, for every \(E\in\mathcal{M}\), either \(\mu(E)=0\) or \(\mu(E)\geq c\).

Let \(\mathcal{H}\) be a Hilbert space and let \(A:\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}\) be a linear map (not assumed bounded). Suppose there exists another linear map \(B:\mathcal{H}\to\mathcal{H}\) (not assumed bounded) so that \(\langle Ax,y\rangle=\langle x,By\rangle\) for all \(x,y\in\mathcal{H}\). Prove that \(A\) and \(B\) are bounded.

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text: State the Closed Graph Theorem and Banach Isomorphism theorem.

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: Using the Banach Isomorphism theorem (or otherwise), prove the Closed Graph Theorem.
